author: DAN KOE
text: You need to read more, and you need to consume less. You need to set aside time to do a workout for the mind, and you need to give it time to recover. Especially now that it's so easy for your cognitive capacity to atrophy. Refuse to become mentally obese.

comments.author: DAN KOE
comments.text: You need to read more, and you need to consume less. You need to set aside time to do a workout for the mind, and you need to give it time to recover. Especially now that it's so easy for your cognitive capacity to atrophy. Refuse to become mentally obese.

comments.author: Emmanuel Anietie
comments.text:
Mental obesity - that's a thing! 

Reading has regenerative power, especially when handling hardcover books. The mind slows down a bit, and the imaginative faculty is awakened.

comments.author: Paul Nash
comments.text:
@grok
 if your brain needs recovery time just like muscles do, how much of what students cram during all nighters actually gets stored vs just disappearing because the brain never got time to recover

comments.author: SoloScale
comments.text:
Reading is great - I usually read on psychology and business however I believe I need to expand what I read.

Would you agree?

comments.author: @th3betterself
comments.text:
You are absolutely right of course, as usual, because the current problem is people consuming too much and reading too little.

But long back, Albert Einstein warned against the idea of reading too much, especially when you are a little older:

Reading after a certain age diverts

comments.author: Blessed Effiong
comments.text: The current surge of content especially on social media platforms makes this consumption issue even worse.

comments.author: Samteic
comments.text: tbh i know people who read a book a week and still can’t think clearly because they never sit with anything long enough to form their own opinion. input isn’t the same as processing

comments.author: Leo Sterling
comments.text: Passive consumption is the path of least resistance and it compounds just like any other habit. Your ability to think deeply, focus, and generate original ideas depends on protecting your inputs as much as your outputs.

comments.author: Demilade Bickersteth
comments.text:
Its so easy to confuse consuming educational content with learning. Consumption expands our awareness, but reading gives time to filter and internalise information so it can be applied.

If there is no change in action, you didn't learn; you were entertained.

comments.author: Muballigur Rahman Choudhury
comments.text: Reading this while eating a burger, which makes this feel a little too on the nose lol. But it’s true. Constant consumption feels like learning, when a lot of it is really just cognitive junk food

comments.author: 198X Observer
comments.text: “Mentally obese” is a brutal phrase. I felt it in my gut, which is ironic because I was reading this while lying in bed, phone 6 inches from my face, having consumed nothing but hot takes for the last hour.

comments.author: Himanshu
comments.text:
This is a great reframe. We talk about physical health constantly but treat the mind like it'll just take care of itself,
meanwhile we're scrolling for hours and calling it rest.

comments.author: Olimp Motivation
comments.text: reading helps, but the consolidation happens in the gaps. your default mode network stitches meaning when you're bored and quiet. if you replace every input with another input, nothing actually sticks.

comments.author: Noah
comments.text: You used to memorize your 2FA code, now it directly appears on your keyboard. Tech is great, but most stay ignorant and end up on the losing side.

comments.author: AIHacksByMK
comments.text: Isn’t reading too much considered consuming too?

comments.author: David Chijioke
comments.text:
Filter what you consume.

Read more on what is peculiar to your purpose.

Give your mind time to recover from already consumed information. 

It isn't always about the volume of the information you consume, every information you find shouldn't end in your mind.
